title: Bevelling and burnishing a leather edge
subtitle: Bevel the corner, wet the fibre, burnish to a hard finish
excerpt: How to finish the cut edge of a leather piece: running an edge beveller to remove the square corner, dampening the exposed fibre, and burnishing with a wooden burnisher to produce a sealed, hard-finished edge.
sourceNotes: Traditional European leatherwork finishing practice. Al Stohlman The Art of Hand Sewing Leather (1977). City & Guilds Leatherwork Level 2.

content.text: A raw cut leather edge has a squared corner and loose surface fibres. Without finishing, the corner catches on surfaces and lifts over time. The
edge bevel
removes the corner in a single controlled pass; burnishing seals the exposed fibres into a smooth, hard edge that holds its shape under use.
Bevelling
Step 1, select the correct beveller size.

Edge bevellers are numbered by the chamfer width they cut: no. 1 for thin leather under 1.5 mm, no. 2 for medium leather 1.5 to 3 mm, no. 3 for heavy leather above 3 mm. Using too large a beveller on thin leather removes too much material and rounds the whole edge rather than cutting a clean chamfer.
Step 2, run the beveller along the top edge.

Hold the beveller at roughly 45 degrees to the surface. Press the groove of the tool against the top corner and pull along the edge in a single smooth pass. Maintain even pressure and speed throughout; lifting or pressing harder at any point changes the chamfer depth and leaves a visible step. Cut the full length in one pass where possible; for long pieces, overlap joins by 10 mm with a running start.
Step 3, bevel the underside edge.

Turn the piece over and repeat on the underside corner. The underside chamfer is typically shallower than the top because the flesh side of the leather is softer than the grain side; reduce pressure slightly.
Burnishing
Step 4, dampen the edge.

Apply a small amount of water, gum tragacanth, or edge kote to the edge using a finger or a scrap of cloth. The surface fibres need to be slightly damp to compress and seal under the burnisher. Do not soak the edge; a thin dampened surface is enough. Allow the moisture to soak in for 30 seconds before burnishing.
Step 5, burnish with the wooden tool.

Run the grooved channel of the leather burnisher along the edge with firm, fast strokes. The friction generates heat that compresses and glazes the exposed fibre. After 20 to 30 strokes the edge should feel hard and smooth with a slight sheen. If the edge stays rough, the leather was not damp enough; re-dampen and repeat.
Step 6, inspect and repeat if needed.

Run a finger along the finished edge. A well-burnished edge feels glassy and shows no loose fibres. Any remaining roughness responds to a second dampening and burnishing pass. Very thin leather may need only one pass; heavy veg-tan leather may need three.
Common mistakes
Beveller removes too much material and rounds the whole edge.
The beveller size is too large for the leather thickness. Move to a no. 1 or no. 2 and test on a scrap piece first.
Burnished edge stays rough after 30 strokes.

The edge was not damp enough, or the burnisher moved too slowly. Re-dampen and burnish with fast, firm strokes. The friction heat is the mechanism; slow strokes do not generate enough heat to seal the fibres.
